outline.preview:

For experimental verification, proteins ranging from 70 to 80 amino acids in length with no conditioning information were generated in 25 steps. outline.heading: Compositionally biased protein generation
outline.preview:
# Compositionally biased protein generation Proteins ranging from 70 to 80 amino acids in length with an amino acid compositional potential were generated in 25 steps. Designs were filtered by AF2 pLDDT > 90, AF2 RMSD to design < 2 Å and SAP score $^{55}$ < 30. The top 10–22 designs were ordered for each upweighted amino acid type (tryptophan, cysteine, valine, histidine and methionine). Pseudocode for the implementa
...[clipped]...

outline.heading: Charge biased protein generation
outline.preview: # Charge biased protein generation Proteins of 50 amino acids in length with charge potentials applied were generated in 25 steps with charge conditioning information. The ground truth charge for each protein was calculated at pH 7.4 by using the Henderson–Hasselbach equation.

outline.heading: Hydrophobic biased protein generation
outline.preview: # Hydrophobic biased protein generation Proteins of 50 amino acids in length with hydrophobic potentials applied were generated in 25 steps with hydrophobicity conditioning information. The ground truth hydropathy index for each design was calculated by summing the hydropathy index for each residue and dividing by the sequence length $^{56}$ .

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Protein denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) are used for de novo generation of protein backbones but are limited in guiding generation with sequence-specific attributes and functional properties. The goal is to develop a sequence-space diffusion model, ProteinGenerator (PG), based on RoseTTAFold, that simultaneously generates protein sequences and structures, enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional protein design.

section_gists.heading: Multistate and functional protein design using RoseTTAFold sequence space diffusion
section_gists.gist: Introduces ProteinGenerator (PG), a sequence-space diffusion model based on RoseTTAFold that generates sequence-structure pairs by iterative denoising. PG overcomes limitations of structure-based diffusion (e.g., RFdiffusion) by enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional design. The model is trained by noising sequences from the PDB and learning to denoise while predicting structure. PG can be guided by desired sequence and structural attributes, and design trajectories can be guided by experimental data.

Hypothesis: carrying out diffusion in sequence space enables guidance using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.
Approach: adapt RoseTTAFold for sequence space diffusion by noising sequences and training to remove noise while imposing structure prediction loss.
methods: Categorical DDPM with scaled one-hot encoding
Training on PDB sequences with progressive noising
Guidance using sequence and structural attributes

Hallucination approaches can be adversarial and require many steps.

section_gists.heading: Categorical DDPM implementation and fine-tuning
section_gists.gist: Describes the implementation of PG: sequences represented as scaled one-hot tensors, embedded via linear layer, and corrupted with Gaussian noise. RoseTTAFold is fine-tuned to predict ground truth sequence-structure pairs from noised sequences, using categorical cross-entropy and FAPE losses. Self-conditioning improves performance. Inference starts from Gaussian noise and iteratively denoises. PG outperforms early hallucination methods in unconditional design accuracy, generates structurally diverse proteins, and scaffolds structural motifs. PG sequence quality (ESM pseudo-perplexity) is comparable to native sequences and better than EvoDiff. Unconditional generation yields proteins with native-like amino acid compositions; 6% of designs have AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å. Experimentally, 32/42 unconditionally generated 70-80 residue proteins were soluble, monomeric, and thermostable.
process_signals: Design choice: scaled one-hot encoding simplifies use of raw sequence-based classifiers for guidance.
Self-conditioning found to improve training and inference.
Sampling x_{t-1} ~ q(x_{t-1}|x_0) more effective than q(x_{t-1}|x_0, x_t).
PG outperforms early hallucination methods in unconditional design accuracy.
RFdiffusion+MPNN performs better on motif scaffolding and unconditional generation of larger proteins.
PG sequence quality (ESM pseudo-perplexity) indistinguishable from native sequences, better than EvoDiff.
Experimental validation: 32/42 soluble, monomeric, thermostable.
methods: Gaussian noise corruption with square root schedule
Fine-tuning RoseTTAFold with categorical cross-entropy and FAPE loss
Inference: predict x0 from xt, noise to x_{t-1}
Fixed motifs featurized with extra token
Secondary structure conditioning via 1D track, 3D coordinates via 2D/3D tracks
metrics: Solubility, monomericity (SEC), secondary structure (CD), thermostability (CD melts)
failures_or_limits: RFdiffusion+MPNN better for motif scaffolding and larger proteins.
Only 6% of unconditional designs have AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å.

section_gists.heading: Design of rare amino acid enriched proteins
section_gists.gist: PG is used to design proteins enriched in rare amino acids (Trp, Cys, Val, His, Met) by biasing sequence positions during denoising. Designs are filtered by AF2 confidence and self-consistency. 68/96 expressed designs were soluble; many were monomeric and thermostable. Cysteine-rich designs formed disulfide bonds without structural conditioning. Valine-rich designs had higher beta-sheet content. PG can also design proteins with specified charge, pI, and hydrophobicity.
process_signals: Advantage of sequence diffusion: sequence-based guiding functions readily implemented.
Guiding procedure: rank positions by frequency of target amino acid, bias top N positions.
Success: designs with 20% composition of rare amino acids, distinct from native sequences.
Cysteine designs formed 3-4 disulfide bonds without structural conditioning.
Valine designs showed expected beta-sheet propensity.
Model reasons over sequence-structure relationships beyond native distributions.
methods: Sequence-based guidance: bias logits toward desired amino acid at top N positions
Experimental: SEC, CD, mass spectrometry (disulfide bonds)
failures_or_limits: Not all designs soluble/monomeric (68/96 soluble, subset monomeric).

section_gists.heading: Design of sequence repeat proteins
section_gists.gist: PG adapted to generate repeat proteins by applying repeat symmetry to the noised sequence distribution at each timestep. Unconditional generation yielded mostly beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning enabled diverse all-alpha, all-beta, and mixed designs. Helical caps added to some designs. 27/74 capped repeats and 10/86 uncapped repeats were soluble and monomeric. Crystal structure of a five-repeat four-helix bundle design showed atomic accuracy (C RMSD 1.38 Å overall, 0.47 Å for asymmetric unit).
process_signals: Previous repeat protein design required pre-specified structural features or expensive MCMC.
PG adapts by applying repeat symmetry during denoising.
Unconditional generation biased toward beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning expanded diversity.
Helical caps improved solubility/monodispersity.
Crystal structure confirms atomic accuracy.
methods: Repeat symmetry applied to noised sequence distribution
failures_or_limits: Low success rate: 27/74 capped, 10/86 uncapped soluble and monomeric.

section_gists.heading: Design of conditionally active peptide cages for membrane lysis
section_gists.gist: PG used to cage bioactive peptides (e.g., melittin) within inert protein scaffolds by fixing the peptide sequence and diffusing the remainder. Unlike LOCKR, no pre-specified structure needed. Designs include furin cleavage site for conditional release. 5/13 designs were soluble, monomeric, helical, and thermostable. Furin cleavage confirmed by band shift and mass spectrometry. Conditional hemolysis assay: RBC lysis only with furin pre-treatment.
process_signals: Challenge: design proteins with conditional activity.
PG approach: fix bioactive peptide sequence, diffuse scaffold, use secondary structure conditioning.
Multiple constraints required increased sampling and filtering.
Success: conditional release and activity demonstrated.
Potential for endosomal escape applications.
methods: Fixed peptide sequence with adjacent furin cleavage site
Secondary structure conditioning (helical bundle, loop for cleavage site)
Experimental: SEC, CD, furin cleavage assay, mass spectrometry, hemolysis assay
failures_or_limits: Required increased sampling and filtering due to multiple constraints.

section_gists.heading: Scaffolding barcode peptide sequences
section_gists.gist: PG scaffolds short barcode sequences (7-14 residues) into proteins, enabling intrinsic barcoding. 64/84 designs expressed, 48/64 monodisperse by SEC. SEC-MS showed close agreement with individual SEC for 41/58 designs. PG can incorporate intrinsic barcodes, reducing need for multiple extrinsic barcodes.
process_signals: Motivation: current barcoding uses flexible fusions, requiring multiple degenerate barcodes.
PG scaffolds barcodes as integral part of structure.
High expression and monodispersity.
SEC-MS validation shows agreement with individual SEC.
methods: Fixed barcode sequence at C-terminus with flanking cleavage sites
failures_or_limits: Not all designs expressed or monodisperse.

section_gists.heading: Multistate design
section_gists.gist: PG enables multistate design by averaging sequence logits between diffusion trajectories with distinct structural constraints. Designed parent-child triples where the same sequence folds to different supersecondary structures when intact vs split. Experimental validation by NMR and crystallography confirmed conformational switching.
process_signals: Multistate design is challenging: energy landscape must have two minima with small free energy difference.
PG approach: logit averaging between trajectories with different structural constraints.
Designed parent-child triples with distinct folds.
Experimental validation by NMR and crystallography.

section_gists.heading: Guidance with experimental data
section_gists.gist: PG used for iterative protein function optimization guided by experimental data. Tested on GB1 fitness landscape: at each round, classifiers trained on previous data guide diffusion. PG outperforms Bayesian optimization baseline, with average and max fitness increasing each round. Advantage due to rich sequence-structure prior in RoseTTAFold.
process_signals: Goal: optimize function in few experimental iterations.
Test case: GB1 benchmark with complete fitness data.
PG approach: gradient-based optimization with classifier guidance.
Outperforms Bayesian optimization with batched UCB.
Rich prior in RoseTTAFold improves performance.
methods: Iterative guidance: train MLP classifiers on experimental fitness, bias sampling via gradients
failures_or_limits: Retrospective comparison limited to datasets with complete fitness data.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: PG generates diverse de novo proteins with sequence constraints. Comparison to RFdiffusion: both use RoseTTAFold, but PG better for soft sequence constraints and multistate design via logit averaging. RFdiffusion better for global structural properties. Filtering required to obtain high-confidence designs. PG advantageous for multistate design and directed evolution campaigns.
process_signals: PG vs RFdiffusion: PG better for soft sequence constraints and multistate design; RFdiffusion better for global structural properties.
Multistate design uniquely enabled by sequence space diffusion and logit averaging.
Filtering needed to increase fraction of confident designs.
PG useful for directed evolution by leveraging sequence-structure prior.
failures_or_limits: Considerable filtering required to obtain sequences satisfying constraints.
RFdiffusion better for unconditional generation of larger proteins and motif scaffolding.

section_gists.heading: Sequence representation
section_gists.gist: Sequences represented as L×20 scaled one-hot tensors (values 1 and -1). Noising follows standard Gaussian forward process with square root noise schedule, which adds more noise at low timesteps to increase task difficulty.
process_signals: Design choice: scaled one-hot encoding for continuous representation of categorical data.
Square root schedule chosen to increase difficulty at low timesteps.

section_gists.heading: Training
section_gists.gist: Training: sample t uniformly, noise x0 to xt, predict x0 and structure y. Losses: categorical cross-entropy on sequence, FAPE and other structure losses, KL loss on x_{t-1}. Self-conditioning implemented. RoseTTAFold allowed 1-3 recycle steps. Secondary structure conditioning added later. Additional tasks: structure prediction and fixed backbone design (30% each) to maintain sequence-structure agreement. Motif scaffolding simulated by unmasking spans.
process_signals: Self-conditioning improves performance.
KL loss stabilizes training.
Multi-task training (diffusion, structure prediction, fixed backbone design) maintains sequence-structure agreement.
Secondary structure conditioning added in later iterations.
methods: FAPE, bond angle, bond length, distogram, lddt losses

section_gists.heading: Inference
section_gists.gist: Inference: from x_t, predict x0 and structure, back-calculate x_{t-1} with self-conditioning. Sampling x_{t-1} ~ q(x_{t-1}|x_0) better than q(x_{t-1}|x_0, x_t). T can be varied; some problems solved in 10 steps. Clamping logits to [-3,3] improves AF2 agreement. x_{t-1} sampled from Gaussian or Gaussian mixture models (GMM2, GMM3).
process_signals: Self-conditioning used during inference.
Sampling from q(x_{t-1}|x_0) more effective.
Logit clamping improves AF2 agreement.
Gaussian mixture sampling increases diversity.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Sequence-space diffusion enables direct sequence-based guidance, overcoming limitations of structure-based diffusion.
RoseTTAFold can be adapted for sequence diffusion by training on noised sequences with structure prediction loss.
PG can design proteins with diverse sequence constraints (rare amino acids, repeats, caged peptides, barcodes) and multistate behavior.
PG can integrate experimental data for iterative functional optimization.
Sequence space diffusion is particularly advantageous for multistate design via logit averaging.
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does PG compare to other sequence diffusion models (e.g., EvoDiff) in more detail?
What are the specific failure modes in designs that did not express or were not monomeric?
How does the success rate of PG scale with protein length and complexity?
Can PG be extended to design larger proteins or more complex multistate systems?
What are the limitations of the current guidance mechanisms?

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Developing a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold that simultaneously generates protein sequences and structures, enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional protein design.

canonical_events.summary: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.
Carrying out diffusion in sequence space could enable guidance using sequence-based features and multistate design.
Sequences represented as scaled one-hot tensors and embedded via linear layer for Gaussian noise corruption.
Square root noise schedule chosen to increase difficulty at low timesteps.
Training with multi-task learning including diffusion (40%), structure prediction (30%), and fixed backbone design (30%) using categorical cross-entropy, FAPE, and KL losses with self-conditioning.
Inference uses self-conditioning and sampling from q(x_{t-1}|x_0) for better performance; logit clamping to [-3,3] improves AF2 agreement.
Unconditional generation yields 6% of designs with AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; 32/42 tested proteins soluble and monomeric.
Rare amino acid enriched proteins designed; 68/96 expressed designs soluble, many monomeric and thermostable; cysteine designs formed 3-4 disulfide bonds without structural conditioning.
Repeat protein generation: unconditional generation biased toward beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning enabled diverse designs; 27/74 capped and 10/86 uncapped soluble and monomeric.
Crystal structure of a five-repeat four-helix bundle design showed atomic accuracy: C RMSD 1.38 Å overall, 0.47 Å for asymmetric unit.
PG used to cage bioactive peptide melittin within an inert protein scaffold by fixing the peptide sequence and diffusing the remainder, with secondary structure conditioning and furin cleavage site.

Experimental validation of melittin cages: 5/13 soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable; furin cleavage confirmed by band shift and mass spectrometry; conditional hemolysis assay showed RBC lysis only with furin pre-treatment.
Designing melittin cages with multiple constraints required increased sampling and filtering.
Scaffolding barcode peptide sequences: 64/84 expressed, 48/64 monodisperse, SEC-MS agreement for 41/58.
Multistate design approach: logit averaging between diffusion trajectories with distinct structural constraints to design parent-child triples.
Experimental characterization of multistate designs: 72 parent-child triples tested; 4 families selected for CD and NMR; NMR structures of MS2 and MS3 parents solved with Cα RMSD 1.06 Å and 1.61 Å.
Iterative design on GB1 fitness landscape: PG outperforms Bayesian optimization in average and maximum fitness over 3 rounds.
Improved performance attributed to rich sequence-structure prior in RoseTTAFold.

source.title: Multistate and functional protein design using RoseTTAFold sequence space diffusion

initial_request.research_problem: Developing a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold that simultaneously generates protein sequences and structures, enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional protein design.
initial_request.known_context: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to enable sequence-based guidance and multistate design in protein diffusion models.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequence-space diffusion could overcome limitations of structure-based models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design sequence representation and diffusion process.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although powerful, structure-based approaches, such as RFdiffusion $^{11}$ and Chroma $^{12}$ , provide limited opportunities to guide protein generation using sequence-based features and to identify sequences with more than one fold and/or function.

We reasoned that carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance of design using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequence representation and noise schedule defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train the model to generate sequences and structures.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-task learning with diffusion, structure prediction, and fixed backbone design will yield a model capable of sequence-structure generation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train RoseTTAFold-based model with multi-task learning (40% diffusion, 30% structure prediction, 30% fixed backbone design) using categorical cross-entropy, FAPE, and KL losses with self-conditioning.

parameters.task_weights: diffusion 40%, structure prediction 30%, fixed backbone design 30%

evolution_trajectory.observation: Model trained with multi-task learning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set up inference procedure with self-conditioning and logit clamping.

evidence.quote_or_span: Along with the standard diffusion task (40% of the time), the model was also challenged with structure prediction (seq2str) and fixed backbone sequence design (30% of the time each).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does unconditional generation produce viable proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Unconditional generation will yield a fraction of high-confidence designs that are soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins unconditionally and filter by AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; experimentally test 42 designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 6% of designs have AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; 32/42 tested proteins soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore sequence-based guidance with rare amino acid enrichment.

evidence.quote_or_span: 6% of designs have AF2 confidence pLDDT > 90 and RMSD < 2 Å
Of the 42 proteins experimentally tested, 32 were soluble and monomeric by SEC
Designs were filtered by AF2 pLDDT > 90 and AF2 RMSD to design < 2 Å for ordering final constructs.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can sequence-based guidance enrich rare amino acids and induce disulfide bonds without structural conditioning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Biasing amino acid composition during generation will yield soluble proteins enriched in rare amino acids, and high cysteine content will form disulfide bonds.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins with biased amino acid composition (tryptophan, cysteine, valine, histidine, methionine) and test expression and solubility.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 68/96 expressed designs soluble; cysteine designs formed 3-4 disulfide bonds without structural conditioning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to repeat protein design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of the expressed designs, 68 were soluble in Escherichia coli
Guiding for high cysteine content at the sequence level resulted in the formation of 3–4 disulfide bonds per protein without any structural conditioning
separate PG trajectories were carried out for each enriched amino acid.
SEC Normalized absorbance
Generated designs were filtered for high AF2 confidence (pLDDT > 90) and self-consistency (RMSD to design < 2 Å), and 96 were selected for experimental characterization.
For broad applicability, we carried out this optimization with standard settings and no extensive hyperparameter turning (96 designs generated and tested per round for three rounds).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG generate diverse repeat proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secondary structure conditioning will enable diverse repeat protein designs beyond beta-solenoids.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate repeat proteins with and without secondary structure conditioning; test solubility and monomericity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Unconditional generation biased toward beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning enabled diverse designs; 27/74 capped and 10/86 uncapped soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate atomic accuracy with crystal structure.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unconditional generation with this approach yielded largely beta-solenoid structures.

To encourage further exploration, we trained PG to condition on secondary structure (Supplementary Fig. 16) and specified secondary structure constraints to yield a wide range of all-alpha, all-beta and mixed alpha/beta designs
Of these, 27 repeats with caps and 10 repeats without helical caps were soluble and monomeric by SEC

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the designs atomically accurate?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed repeat protein structure will match the crystal structure with high accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Solve crystal structure of a five-repeat four-helix bundle design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C RMSD 1.38 Å overall, 0.47 Å for asymmetric unit.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to cage bioactive peptides.

evidence.quote_or_span: We solved the crystal structure of a five-repeat unit design composed of a four-helix bundle asymmetric unit and found the design to have atomic accuracy: the C RMSD of design to the crystal structure was 1.38 Å for the whole structure and 0.47 Å for the asymmetric unit
Diffraction quality crystals formed in a mixture of 0.1 M PCB buffer (pH 4) and 25% PEG 1500.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG cage bioactive peptides for conditional activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fixing a bioactive peptide sequence and diffusing the remainder with secondary structure conditioning and a furin cleavage site will yield a caged peptide that is released upon protease cleavage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design melittin cages by fixing melittin sequence, diffusing scaffold, and including furin cleavage site.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Melittin cage designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Experimentally validate melittin cages.

evidence.quote_or_span: We used PG to address this challenge by scaffolding bioactive peptide sequences within an inert protein cage (Fig. 4a) by designating a region of the protein chain (usually at the N or C terminus) to be fixed at the bioactive peptide sequence and freely diffusing the remainder of the sequence.
Proteins of 155 amino acids in length were generated in 25 steps with spans of helical DSSP conditioning to encourage the model to generate helical bundles and cleavage loops.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the designed cages express, fold, and release melittin upon furin cleavage?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A subset of designs will be soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable, and show conditional hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express and characterize 13 designs; test furin cleavage and hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 5/13 soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable; furin cleavage confirmed; conditional hemolysis (P=0.002, P=0.005).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation of increased sampling and filtering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable
Upon addition of furin protease, a band shift from -18 kD to -15 kD indicated cleavage of our designed melittin cage
Mass spectrometry analysis confirmed release of intact melittin peptide
Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable
